page: Vultr vcg-l40s-16c-180g-48vram is a Cloud GPU server configured with an Intel x86_64 processor featuring 16 physical cores and 16 vCPUs, operating with a 1.0 thread-per-core ratio. The system includes 180.0 GB of system memory, yielding 11.25 GB of RAM per core. For hardware acceleration, it integrates a single NVIDIA L40S GPU from the Ada Lovelace architecture, equipped with 48 GB of dedicated VRAM. Local storage is provided by a 1200 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

A key architectural consideration is the shared CPU allocation model, where the 16 vCPUs share physical host resources. Additionally, the CPU flags indicate that advanced instruction sets, such as Advanced Vector Extensions (AVX, AVX-512), Advanced Matrix Extensions (AMX), and hardware-assisted cryptography (AES, SHA_NI), are not exposed or available on this instance type. Hyperthreading is also disabled, as indicated by the single thread per core configuration.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this server balances a high-capacity 48 GB GPU frame buffer with a shared CPU model, making it a viable option for workloads that require substantial GPU memory and compute but do not demand dedicated, continuous CPU execution. The hardware profile is suited for GPU-accelerated tasks such as medium-scale LLM inference, graphics rendering, and machine learning training workloads that fit within the 48 GB VRAM envelope and 180.0 GB system memory.
description: Vultr vcg-l40s-16c-180g-48vram is a Cloud GPU server equipped with an Intel x86_64 processor featuring 16 physical cores, 16 vCPUs, and 180.0 GB of system memory. The server features a single NVIDIA L40S GPU based on the Ada Lovelace architecture with 48 GB of VRAM, alongside 1200 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. A key architectural tradeoff is the shared CPU allocation and the absence of advanced CPU instruction sets like AVX-512 and AMX. With 11.25 GB of RAM per core and a high-capacity GPU, this configuration provides qualitative cost efficiency for GPU-heavy workloads that do not require dedicated CPU performance. It is suited for machine learning inference, model training, and graphics rendering tasks.
